SANDIGANBAYAN ORDERS SEIZURE OF 16 PROPERTIES OWNED BY FORMER CONGRESSMAN ZALDY CO IN P289 MILLION FLOOD CONTROL GRAFT CASE

The anti-graft court is now moving against Zaldy Co's assets.

The Sandiganbayan Fifth Division has issued a writ of preliminary attachment against 16 real estate properties belonging to former Ako Bicol party-list Representative Elizaldy "Zaldy" Co and his wife, Mylene, in connection with the allegedly substandard P289 million flood control project in Naujan, Oriental Mindoro.

The order allows the court sheriff to attach enough of Co's property in the Philippines to satisfy a potential civil liability amounting to P215 million, should Co be convicted.

 THE LEGAL BASIS

The court granted the government prosecutors' ex-parte motion, citing Rule 127 of the Rules of Court on Provisional Remedies in Criminal Cases.

The Sandiganbayan said the rule applies when a criminal action involves money or property "embezzled, fraudulently misapplied, or converted" by a public officer or any person in a fiduciary capacity.

"The present case squarely falls within the ambit of Section 2(b), Rule 127, as it is predicated on a claim involving public funds allegedly embezzled, fraudulently misapplied, or converted by the accused Department of Public Works and Highways officers in conspiracy with the accused Co," the court stated.

The Sandiganbayan further cited what it described as fraud "inherent in a violation of Section 3(e) of RA No. 3019, the Anti-Graft and Corrupt Practices Act, when evident bad faith is alleged, as in the present case."

The prosecution confirmed in its Affidavit of Merit that there is no other sufficient security for the claim.

"Unless his properties are immediately attached, the State may be unable to satisfy any future judgment, as accused Co may easily alienate or conceal his remaining assets in the Philippines," the court said.

 THE FLOOD CONTROL PROJECT

The case traces back to a P289.5 million road dike along the Mag-Asawang Tubig River in Naujan, Oriental Mindoro. The project was supposed to be a flood control measure. What government inspectors found when they examined it was something else.

On September 9, 2025, DPWH Secretary Vince Dizon and Oriental Mindoro Governor Bonz Dolor personally inspected the project site. According to the findings reported by the Philstar and confirmed by Dizon's own testimony at the Sandiganbayan, the steel sheet piles used in the dike were "significantly below the required standards."

The Commission on Audit's Region 4B auditor, Victoriano Acosta, testified during bail hearings that COA issued a notice of disallowance worth P84 million out of the P289 million budget after determining the project was "constructed not in accordance with plans and specifications," according to GMA News.

 CO AS A FUGITIVE

Co left the Philippines on August 6, 2025, approximately three months before the Office of the Ombudsman filed the first criminal charges against him at the Sandiganbayan on November 18, 2025.

The Sandiganbayan has declared Co a fugitive from justice. On March 5, the court denied his motion for reconsideration of that status, calling it both filed beyond the reglementary period and lacking in merit, according to a GMA News report.

Co is one of 16 individuals facing charges. The others include officials from the DPWH-Mimaropa regional office and board members of Sun Corporation, a construction firm owned by Co.

The charges filed by Assistant Ombudsman Aserico Clavano include malversation of public funds through falsification of documents under the Revised Penal Code and violation of Section 3(e) of the Anti-Graft and Corrupt Practices Act. For Co specifically, the Ombudsman also filed charges under Section 3(b) of the same law, concerning claims of receiving undue financial or material advantages.

 THE WIDER FLOOD CONTROL RECKONING

This case is one piece of a much larger wave. The flood control corruption scandals that erupted in 2025 brought hundreds of thousands of Filipinos into the streets, triggered the resignation of former House Speaker Martin Romualdez, and led to a nationwide audit of flood control contracts.

Co's case was the first flood control-related criminal case filed at the Sandiganbayan, according to the Ombudsman. Other cases remain under preliminary investigation.

DPWH Secretary Dizon has stated publicly that the evidence against Co is "robust" and "unequivocal." During an impromptu interview at the Sandiganbayan in January, Dizon said: "The evidence will clearly demonstrate that the project was subpar, that former Rep. Zaldy Co and officials from the DPWH colluded to execute a deficient project to misappropriate funds meant for our citizens."

Co's counsel, lawyer Ruy Rondain, has not yet publicly responded to the writ of preliminary attachment, according to GMA News.

MOJTABA KHAMENEI IS NOW SUPREME LEADER OF IRAN AND HIS FIRST ACT WAS TO ORDER MISSILES FIRED AT ISRAEL AND THE GULF

The succession is no longer a question.

Shortly after midnight on March 9, Iranian state broadcaster IRIB confirmed what had been expected for days: Mojtaba Khamenei, the 56-year-old son of the late Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, has been named Iran's new supreme leader. The Assembly of Experts, Iran's 88-member clerical body responsible for selecting the top leader, made the decision.

Within hours, Iran's president, the Revolutionary Guards, and the armed forces pledged full allegiance to Mojtaba Khamenei, according to Al Jazeera.

Then came the missiles.

 THE FIRST STRIKES UNDER THE NEW LEADER

IRIB posted a photo on Telegram showing a projectile with the words "At Your Command, Sayyid Mojtaba." The caption read: "Iran fires first wave of missiles under the leadership of Ayatollah Seyyed Mojtaba Khamenei toward occupied territories."

Missiles and drones were launched not only toward Israel but across the Gulf. Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Kuwait, Bahrain, the United Arab Emirates, and Iraq all reported incoming projectiles overnight, according to Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty.

In Bahrain, an Iranian drone attack wounded 32 civilians, four of them seriously, including children, according to Bahrain's Health Ministry. Witnesses later reported thick smoke rising from the direction of Bahrain's Bapco oil refinery, which had already been damaged by Iranian missiles four days earlier, according to Reuters.

Explosions rocked the Qatari capital, Doha. The Saudi Foreign Ministry issued a statement warning that continued Iranian attacks would have "serious impact" on relations "now and in the future," condemning what it called "blatant Iranian attacks against a number of Arab countries and the resulting targeting of civilians, vital facilities, and diplomatic missions."

Israel said one woman was moderately injured in the missile barrage before the all-clear was given.

 ISRAEL KILLS QUDS FORCE COMMANDERS IN BEIRUT

Hours before the new leader's appointment, the Israeli military announced it had carried out a strike on a hotel room in Beirut's Raouche district, killing several senior commanders of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps' Quds Force.

According to sources cited by Saudi-owned Al Hadath and reported by Iran International, five Iranian members of the Quds Force's Lebanon branch were killed, including the branch's financial officer, intelligence officer, and senior operatives handling Lebanon and Palestine operations.

Lebanon's state-run National News Agency reported four fatalities and ten injuries. The Israeli military said the commanders were "operating to advance terror attacks against the state of Israel and its civilians."

 THE SCHOOL, THE CHILDREN, THE INVESTIGATION

One of the gravest reported incidents on Day 10 involved an elementary school in Iran.

Iranian state media reported that a strike on a school killed at least 168 children. CNN reported that the incident is now under US investigation, with video footage suggesting that a US airstrike targeting a nearby naval base may have caused the casualties. No official confirmation or denial has been issued by the US military as of this writing.

This figure remains unverified by independent organizations. As required by MCT's reporting standards, the source of this casualty claim is Iranian state media.

 THE NUMBERS SO FAR

According to Iran's health ministry, cited by Al Jazeera, more than 1,300 people have been killed in Iran since the war began on February 28.

The broader toll, as compiled from multiple wire services and government statements: 1,332 dead in Iran, 339 in Lebanon, 11 in Israel, 7 US military personnel, 3 in the UAE, 2 in Iraq, 2 in Saudi Arabia, and 1 across Oman and Bahrain, according to figures attributed to the respective governments and media outlets.

 OIL AT $115 AND THE PHILIPPINE PRICE TAG

Oil prices eclipsed $115 per barrel on Monday, according to the Associated Press. That is the highest level since the 2022 surge following Russia's invasion of Ukraine.

For the Philippines, which imports nearly 90 percent of its oil from the Middle East, every dollar added to the barrel price translates directly to the pump. Diesel at most Philippine stations is set to rise by P17 to P24 per liter starting Tuesday, March 10, in staggered hikes confirmed by Energy Secretary Sharon Garin.

The Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly 20 percent of the world's oil transits, remains effectively shut to commercial shipping. Reuters reported that tanker crossings dropped to zero by midweek. Lloyd's List reported traffic down 81 percent, with just one crude oil tanker crossing on March 1 and no LNG carriers at all. War risk insurance for the region expired on March 5, and no new tankers have been willing to enter the Middle East Gulf, according to Lloyd's List.

President Donald Trump called the oil price surge a "small price to pay" to eliminate Iran's nuclear threat. The US State Department ordered nonessential diplomatic personnel and families at the embassy in Riyadh to leave Saudi Arabia, according to the New York Times.

P52.8 BILLION — THAT IS WHAT SENATOR HONTIVEROS SAYS THE GOVERNMENT NEEDS TO SURVIVE THE OIL CRISIS

Where does the money come from when diesel is about to cost more than a meal?

Senate Deputy Majority Leader Risa Hontiveros walked into a press conference on Monday, March 9, and laid out a number that puts the scale of the Philippine oil emergency into sharp relief: P52.8 billion. That is her office's estimate for the emergency supplemental budget the country needs to absorb the economic damage heading its way from the Middle East.

The proposal is divided into three parts.

P12 billion for transport subsidies. P2.8 billion for agricultural subsidies. And at least P38 billion for the emergency repatriation and reintegration of overseas Filipino workers.

 THE TRANSPORT GAP

Hontiveros started with the math on transport.

The Department of Energy currently has no new funds in the 2026 national budget earmarked for a fuel subsidy. What remains is approximately P2.5 billion in continuing funds left over from 2025.

That P2.5 billion is already inadequate. Hontiveros's office projects the real cost burden at P12 billion if the average fuel price increase exceeds P10 per liter over three months.

"If we know that there will be billions of dollars in the gap, we cannot be so small in the budget," she said.

This projection arrives one day before the first wave of staggered fuel price hikes begins on Tuesday, March 10, when diesel prices at most stations are expected to jump by P17 to P24 per liter. Energy Secretary Sharon Garin confirmed the increases will be spread across seven days, company by company, rather than imposed in one shock.

 AGRICULTURE AND FERTILIZER

The second piece of the package targets agriculture.

Hontiveros said rising oil prices will drag fertilizer costs upward, a chain reaction well documented in past energy shocks. Her office estimates the additional cost exposure for the agricultural sector at P2.8 billion.

What does the Department of Agriculture currently have on hand? P150 million.

"The projected burden is at P2.8 billion, and all the sector has is P150 million. That is not protection. Far from it," she said.

She also pointed to P2.5 billion in farm machinery funds under the Philippine Center for Postharvest Development and Mechanization that could be repurposed toward agricultural subsidies as a stopgap.

 THE P38 BILLION OFW QUESTION

The largest single item in Hontiveros's proposal is the P38 billion emergency fund for OFWs.

The Department of Migrant Workers currently has P1.76 billion in its emergency fund. Hontiveros argued that amount is sufficient only for limited, incremental repatriation. If the Middle East crisis escalates further and a large-scale evacuation becomes necessary, the government will need to cover not just flights and logistics but income support, job placement, and reintegration programs for returning workers.

"If the situation worsens, large-scale evacuation and reintegration support will be needed. That should include income support and employment programs for returning OFWs, which necessitates the P38 billion funding," she said.

The DMW on Saturday, March 8, designated the Persian Gulf, the Strait of Hormuz, and the Gulf of Oman as warlike operations areas. Filipino seafarers assigned to vessels operating in those waters can now legally refuse deployment. As of Monday, the Strait of Hormuz remains functionally shut to commercial shipping, with zero tanker crossings recorded since midweek, according to Reuters.

 WHERE THE MONEY WOULD COME FROM

Hontiveros acknowledged there is no existing legislation for the supplemental budget but argued the government has options.

She identified approximately P70 billion from discontinued flood control projects in 2025 as a potential funding source. Those projects, many of which are at the center of the ongoing national corruption investigations involving contractors like Zaldy Co, were halted after public outrage over shoddy infrastructure and graft allegations.

She also suggested phasing obligations on large-scale infrastructure projects to free up cash in the near term.

"Although there is currently no legislation for the supplemental budget, subsidies can commence, even if only partially," she said.

 THE BIGGER LEGISLATIVE PICTURE

Hontiveros's proposal sits alongside the separate push for emergency powers that President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. requested from Congress last week. That request would grant the executive temporary authority to suspend or reduce excise taxes on petroleum products.

Marikina Representative Miro Quimbo filed House Bill No. 8257 last Tuesday to amend the National Internal Revenue Code, adding a provision that would let the President suspend the fuel excise tax on the finance secretary's recommendation. Senators Joel Villanueva and Bam Aquino filed their own Senate versions.

The House Ways and Means Committee convened a briefing on March 9 to discuss these measures. Speaker Bojie Dy has publicly called for swift legislative action to shield Filipinos from the oil price shock.

But not everyone agrees.

The Makabayan bloc, including ACT Teachers party-list Representative Antonio Tinio, Gabriela party-list Representative Sarah Elago, and Kaban party-list Representative Renee Louise Co, rejected the emergency powers approach entirely. They argued that Congress already has pending bills to permanently eliminate the excise tax and VAT on petroleum products. Granting the President discretionary suspension authority, the bloc said, merely "serves as a distraction, keeping the public dependent on concessions from the President."

THE EXACT NUMBERS ARE IN: DIESEL WILL JUMP UP TO P24.25 PER LITER THIS WEEK AND THE GOVERNMENT JUST SAID IT COULD REACH P96

Department of Energy Secretary Sharon Garin stood before reporters on Monday afternoon and announced what fuel retailers will charge starting Tuesday. The numbers, broken down company by company, represent the largest single-week diesel price increase in Philippine history.

Shell Pilipinas will impose the steepest hike at P24.25 per liter, staggered across three days: P14.55 on Tuesday, P7.30 on Wednesday, and P2.40 on Thursday. Seaoil follows at P21 to P23 per liter, phased across two increments. Total will add P20.20, split evenly over two days. Petron increases by P19.20, starting at P11.60 on Tuesday with additional daily adjustments. Jetti Petroleum raises prices by P19, beginning with P4 on Tuesday and P3 per day for the next five days. Chevron will apply the smallest increase at P17.50, spread across seven daily increments of P2.50 each.

Gasoline will rise by P7 to P13 per liter, also starting Tuesday.

At prevailing diesel prices of P35 to P36 per liter, the full increases push pump prices to between P52 and P60 per liter at major stations. The DOE emphasized that it does not set prices directly under Republic Act 8479, the Downstream Oil Industry Deregulation Act, but has been monitoring compliance and has already issued show-cause orders to gas stations that adjusted prices ahead of schedule.

 THE P96 SCENARIO

Hours before Garin's press conference, a senior official from the Department of Economy, Planning, and Development laid out two scenarios for where prices could go from here.

Scenario 1 assumes the Middle East conflict remains contained and the Strait of Hormuz partially reopens. Under this model, diesel could reach P74.22 per liter in March and settle at P67.33 in April. Suspending the P6 excise tax on diesel would lower those figures to P67.50 and P60.61, respectively.

Scenario 2 assumes Iran maintains closure of the Strait of Hormuz and global oil prices climb to US$140 per barrel. In that case, diesel would reach P96.76 per liter in March. Even with excise tax suspension, it would only drop to P90.04. For April, the projection drops slightly to P91.19 without suspension, or P84.47 with it.

Gasoline under Scenario 2 could reach P69.20 per liter, potentially falling to P59 with excise tax suspension.

DEPDev also projected inflation impacts. Scenario 1 pushes inflation to between 4.4 and 5.1 percent in March, well above the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas target range of 2 to 4 percent. Scenario 2 could drive inflation as high as 7.5 percent, a level not seen since the height of the post-pandemic price spiral in 2023.

 POLICE NOW PATROLLING GAS STATIONS

On the same day, the Philippine National Police formalized its role in the fuel crisis.

PNP Chief Police General Jose Melencio Nartatez Jr. ordered all chiefs of police and unit commanders to include gas stations in regular patrol routes, following a direct request from the DOE. The Criminal Investigation and Detection Group, Maritime Group, and Highway Patrol Group have been separately tasked with conducting operations against possible hoarding.

Nartatez said intelligence units are monitoring warehouses. "We will arrest anyone caught illegally withholding supply to spike prices," he stated.

The PNP has also coordinated with the Department of Trade and Industry and local government units to conduct spot checks on retailers, verifying compliance with the DOE's directive that no price changes occur until the scheduled Tuesday adjustments.

There are approximately 14,000 gasoline stations across the Philippines.

 THE SCALE OF WHAT IS HAPPENING

The previous record for a single-week diesel price increase was P13.15 per liter, set on March 15, 2022, during the early weeks of the Russia-Ukraine war. Shell's P24.25 increase nearly doubles that figure.

The Philippines imports approximately 90 percent of its oil, with the majority sourced from the Middle East. Global crude surpassed US$100 per barrel over the weekend, driven by the US-Israel military campaign against Iran now entering its 10th day and the near-total shutdown of tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, which normally carries 20 percent of the world's oil and gas supply.

The government's response so far includes a shift to a four-day workweek for government offices, a plan to seek emergency powers from Congress to suspend or reduce the excise tax on fuel, targeted fuel subsidies for public utility vehicle drivers and fishermen, free MRT and LRT rides proposed by several legislators, and orders for oil companies to stagger their price increases rather than impose them all at once.

Congress returns on Monday with the emergency powers bill expected to be a priority. Speaker Faustino Dy III has ordered the House to fast-track deliberations. Several senators, including Joel Villanueva, Bam Aquino, and Sherwin Gatchalian, have filed their own versions of the bill.

Finance Secretary Frederick Go has cautioned that suspending all excise taxes on fuel for a year could cost the government approximately P300 billion in lost revenue.

DE LIMA AND A MAGSAYSAY AWARDEE WALK INTO THE NBI TO FILE CHARGES AGAINST THE 18 WHO ACCUSED THEM

Eighteen people introduced themselves on national television as former Marines. Their lawyer, Levito Baligod, told the press they had served as bagmen, personally delivering P805 billion in alleged kickbacks from fraudulent flood control projects to politicians across both chambers of Congress and the presidential palace.

Two of the names on that list were Leila de Lima and Fr. Flaviano "Flavie" Villanueva.

On Monday morning, March 9, De Lima and Villanueva showed up at the NBI Cybercrime Division together. They filed a cyber libel complaint against all 18 individuals and Baligod. On the same day, a separate perjury complaint was filed at the Department of Justice.

De Lima is now a House representative under the Mamamayang Liberal party-list. She spent six years detained on drug charges widely regarded as politically motivated, was eventually acquitted, and returned to public office. Villanueva, an SVD priest and founder of the Arnold Janssen Kalinga Center, won the 2025 Ramon Magsaysay Award for his decades of work with homeless youth and families affected by the drug war.

Their joint appearance at the NBI was deliberate, coordinated, and pointed.

 THE ALLEGATIONS THEY ARE FIGHTING

Baligod's group claimed, in a press conference, that De Lima and Villanueva were among those who received cash from former Ako Bicol party-list Representative Elizaldy "Zaldy" Co in connection with anomalous flood control projects.

But the story started falling apart almost immediately.

Baligod himself acknowledged he made a mistake including De Lima in the list. He then amended his account, saying she was not given a suitcase of cash but was supposedly present during a meeting where money was handed to her in a paper bag.

The Philippine Navy confirmed that four of the 18 individuals were never affiliated with the service. A significant number of the rest had received dishonorable discharges.

Ombudsman Jesus Crispin Remulla, whose office authorized the NBI to investigate the claims, said the actions of the 18 appeared to be politically motivated. He noted that six of the individuals currently face murder charges.

 WHY THE NBI AND DOJ ARE BOTH INVOLVED

De Lima and Villanueva pursued two separate legal tracks simultaneously.

The cyber libel complaint went to the NBI because the allegations were amplified online. Baligod's press conference was livestreamed and clips were circulated widely on social media, which brings the claims under the Cybercrime Prevention Act.

The perjury complaint was filed at the DOJ because the 18 individuals executed sworn affidavits asserting their allegations under oath. If those affidavits contain false statements, the signatories face criminal prosecution.

The dual filing is strategic. It forces two different government agencies to independently evaluate the credibility of the same set of claims.

 THE BROADER INFORMATION WAR

This is not happening in a vacuum.

Palace spokesperson Claire Castro dismissed the allegations made by the 18 as a "lousy script." The NBI, empowered by the Ombudsman, issued subpoenas to all 18 individuals on March 5. And the timing of Baligod's press conference, coming in the middle of the ongoing flood control scandal investigations, the Sara Duterte impeachment proceedings, and the ICC Duterte case, has drawn scrutiny from multiple political observers.

On social media, threads analyzing the political networks behind the press conference have circulated widely. Several accounts noted connections between Baligod's group and political operators historically aligned with the Arroyo-era political infrastructure, which analysts say has migrated toward the Sara Duterte camp.

None of this has been independently verified by credible journalism outlets. What has been verified is the following: the Ombudsman himself questioned the motives of the group. The Navy questioned their credentials. And two of the people they accused walked into the NBI on Monday to put their names on formal complaints.

De Lima, speaking briefly before entering the NBI building, said she has been through this before.

"They have done it before. They are doing it again. I will not allow them to tarnish my name again, whatever agenda they have."

 WHAT HAPPENS NEXT

The NBI will evaluate the cyber libel complaint against the 18 individuals and Baligod. The DOJ will assess the perjury complaint.

If the NBI and DOJ find sufficient grounds, charges could be filed at the Ombudsman or in court. If they do not, the complaints may be dismissed.

Either way, the formal legal filings shift the burden. The 18 now have to respond to sworn complaints, not just amplify press conference claims.

THE PHILIPPINE PESO CRASHES TO 59.7 PER DOLLAR AND THE PNP IS NOW ARRESTING FUEL HOARDERS

The peso opened Monday at its worst level in history.

In early morning trading on March 9, the Philippine peso dropped to an intraday low of 59.7 per US dollar, 70 centavos weaker than its previous close and well past the former intraday record of 59.50. It also shattered the all-time closing low of 59.46.

The collapse was not gradual. Just one week ago, analysts at MUFG Global Markets Research were warning the peso could slide to a range of 58.50 to 59 if oil stayed elevated. Oil did not just stay elevated. It blew past $100 a barrel. And the peso did not just slide. It cratered.

The Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas now faces a dilemma it has been trying to avoid for weeks. Governor Eli Remolona Jr. had already signaled that a rate hike was on the table if oil prices stayed above $90. Oil is now above $107. Brent crude closed last week at $109.46. The BSP's entire easing cycle, which brought the policy rate down to 4.25 percent through February, is functionally over.

 THE PNP ENTERS THE FUEL CRISIS

While the peso was falling, the Philippine National Police was gearing up for a different kind of enforcement.

PNP Chief Gen. Jose Melencio Nartatez Jr. ordered all territorial and support unit commanders to assist the Department of Energy in running down oil price manipulators and fuel hoarders. Intelligence units have been deployed to monitor warehouses for illegal stockpiling, and Nartatez issued a direct warning.

"We will arrest anyone caught illegally withholding supply to spike prices," Nartatez said.

This goes beyond the DOE's earlier actions. On March 5, the DOE flagged "possible profiteering and hoarding" in the downstream oil industry and coordinated with the Department of Interior and Local Government and the PNP for immediate action. By March 8, DOE Secretary Sharon Garin issued a public warning against unauthorized or premature fuel price increases, saying all adjustments must follow the established schedule set by President Marcos.

"There is no need for panic buying. The country possesses a sufficient fuel supply," Garin said.

But reports of daily price hikes at gas stations throughout the previous week tell a different story. Rep. Rolando Valeriano said instances of fuel hoarding were already evident on social media, documented by citizens filming gas stations in real time.

Nartatez instructed all territorial units to include gas stations in their routine patrols. Coordination protocols have been set up with the DOE, the DTI, and local government units for spot checks on retailers.

 WHERE THE PRESSURE IS COMING FROM

The chain reaction is straightforward.

The Strait of Hormuz, which normally handles about 20 percent of the world's seaborne oil and a fifth of global liquefied natural gas trade, remains effectively closed to most commercial traffic. UK-based marine insurers cancelled war risk coverage for the Persian Gulf and the strait effective March 5, and without insurance, most vessels cannot dock at ports or secure financing.

Iraq's oil production from its three main southern oilfields has collapsed to 1.3 million barrels per day, down 70 percent from 4.3 million barrels per day before the Iran conflict began on February 28, according to Reuters estimates. The United Arab Emirates and Kuwait have both started cutting output as onshore storage fills up with crude that cannot be shipped through the strait.

On March 7, the Malta-flagged oil tanker Prima was struck by an IRGC drone after allegedly ignoring transit warnings, according to Iran's semi-official Tasnim News Agency. Reuters reports at least eight vessels have been attacked in the area since the war began.

For the Philippines, which imports more than 90 percent of its fuel, the arithmetic is punishing.

 INFLATION IS NOW THE BIGGEST DOMESTIC RISK

February's headline inflation came in at 2.4 percent year-on-year. That was within the BSP's two-to-four percent target range. But multiple economists now expect that number to climb sharply.

The Bank of the Philippine Islands warned that inflation could approach the upper end of the BSP's target range in coming months, driven primarily by rice supply constraints and surging global oil prices. ING's senior economist Bhargava noted that the government could invoke the TRAIN Law provision that suspends fuel excise taxes when global prices exceed $80 per barrel, but even that may not be enough to absorb the kind of increases now being projected.

The next fuel price hike, expected Tuesday March 10, will be one of the largest single-week increases in Philippine history. The country's previous record for a one-week diesel hike was P13.15 per liter, set on March 15, 2022, during the early days of the Russia-Ukraine war. Current projections suggest this week's adjustment could exceed that.

 THE FULL PICTURE

The peso at 59.7. The PNP patrolling gas stations and threatening to arrest hoarders. The DOE ordering compliance with price schedules. Congress debating emergency powers to suspend excise taxes. The BSP staring at a rate hike it was hoping to avoid.

All of these are downstream consequences of a single chokepoint: the Strait of Hormuz. And that chokepoint remains closed to most traffic with no clear timeline for reopening.

For the 2.4 million Filipino workers in the Middle East, the Department of Migrant Workers has set up evacuation criteria prioritizing those nearest to missile and drone strike zones. Over 2,000 OFWs have received assistance so far. A P2 billion OFW business loan program through the Small Business Corporation is set to begin accepting applications on March 12, offering loans up to P5 million with no collateral required and a one-year grace period on payments.

The BSP meets next on its scheduled policy date. But at 59.7 to the dollar and oil above $107, the question is whether they can afford to wait that long.

ASIAN STOCK MARKETS CRASH AS OIL BREACHES $100 A BARREL AND THE BSP'S RATE HIKE TRIGGER IS NOW LIVE

Nikkei 225 down 6.2 percent. Kospi down 6.1 percent. S&P 500 futures down 1.9 percent. Australia and New Zealand both down more than 3 percent.

That was Monday morning across Asian trading floors.

When oil markets reopened on Sunday evening, Brent crude surged to $109.46 a barrel and West Texas Intermediate climbed to $107.37 — both more than 18 percent above Friday's closing prices, according to the Associated Press. Brent briefly crossed $110, the highest since the early months of the Russia-Ukraine war in 2022.

Japan's Nikkei 225 dropped 6.2 percent to 52,184.41 shortly after trading opened, according to the AP. South Korea's Kospi sank 6.1 percent to 5,180.19. Shares in Australia and New Zealand fell more than 3 percent.

In the United States, stock futures for the S&P 500, Dow Jones Industrial Average, and Nasdaq Composite each dropped about 1.9 percent on Sunday evening, according to The New York Times.

Oil is now roughly 50 percent more expensive than it was before the US and Israel began military operations against Iran on February 28, according to the Times.

 THE BSP THRESHOLD HAS BEEN CROSSED

Here is where the numbers turn into a direct Philippine problem.

Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas Governor Eli Remolona Jr. told CNBC over the weekend that the central bank may have to reverse course and raise interest rates if crude oil climbs to $100 a barrel and the US dollar keeps strengthening against the peso.

Oil is now at $107.

The BSP has cut rates a cumulative 225 basis points since August 2024, bringing its key policy rate down to 4.25 percent. That easing cycle was designed to support an economy weakened by slow government spending and the aftershocks of the flood control corruption scandal.

Nomura, the Japanese investment bank, has already revised its Philippine inflation forecast upward from 2.5 percent to 3.2 percent for 2026 and retracted the final 25-basis-point rate cut it had predicted for April.

February inflation jumped to a 13-month high of 2.4 percent. The peso weakened to P58.44 against the US dollar. Remolona warned in the same interview that OFW remittances — which normally cushion peso weakness — could be disrupted if Filipino workers in the Middle East face repatriation.

The conditions he described as hypothetical are no longer hypothetical. Every single one of them has arrived.

 WHAT DROVE THE OVERNIGHT SURGE

Crude had already risen 36 percent (US oil) and 28 percent (Brent) over the past week. The overnight spike was driven by the formal naming of Mojtaba Khamenei as Iran's new supreme leader on Sunday — a move that signaled hardline continuity and eliminated any near-term prospect of de-escalation, according to Anadolu Agency.

Shipping through the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively shut down since the first days of the war. The Strait normally carries about one-fifth of the world's crude oil. There is no clear indication it will normalize soon, according to The New York Times.

The coalition expanded airstrikes to Iranian oil facilities for the first time over the weekend. The Israel Defense Forces struck two oil refineries and two storage sites, according to the Institute for the Study of War. CENTCOM has reported over 3,000 strikes in Iran since February 28.

 FUEL HIKE EVE — AGAIN

The Philippine fuel price adjustment is expected to take effect on Tuesday, March 10.

The DOE's minimum projections from Friday's full five-day trading data: diesel up P19.62 per liter, gasoline up P10.43, kerosene up P24.92, according to Manila Bulletin. Those are minimums. Industry estimates put diesel as high as P22 to P24 per liter.

If those numbers hold even partially, diesel could approach P94 per liter at the pump. The previous single-week adjustment record — P13.15 per liter for diesel, set in March 2022 during the Russia-Ukraine war — will be shattered.

Energy Secretary Sharon Garin has been negotiating with oil companies for staggered implementation, but no company has publicly committed to a specific schedule.

Oil companies are expected to release their official price advisories today, March 9. Under DOE rules, they must notify the department at least six hours before implementing any price increase.

 THE PHILIPPINE TRADING FLOOR

The PSEi closed at 6,307.84 on March 3, down 2.13 percent. It recovered slightly to 6,380.53 on March 5. After what happened overnight in Tokyo, Seoul, and US futures, the index faces its own reckoning when it opens under the new four-day workweek schedule.

Japan holds emergency oil reserves equivalent to 254 days of domestic consumption and is considering releasing part of its strategic reserves, according to Moneycontrol. The Philippines has a two-month supply, according to the DOE. Japan imports 95 percent of its oil from the Middle East. The Philippines imports nearly 90 percent from the same region.

US President Donald Trump dismissed the oil surge on social media Sunday evening, writing that short-term oil prices are "a very small price to pay for U.S.A., and World, Safety and Peace."

For the Philippines — a country that produces almost no oil of its own, sends hundreds of thousands of workers to the Middle East, and is about to absorb the largest fuel price increase in its deregulated history — the question is how long "short term" lasts.

54 GAS STATIONS NOW FACE GOVERNMENT ACTION FOR ILLEGAL PRICE HIKES AS KUWAIT SHUTS DOWN OIL PRODUCTION AND MARCOS SENDS EMERGENCY POWERS REQUEST TO CONGRESS

The Department of Energy has issued 54 show-cause orders against gasoline stations across the Philippines for allegedly raising pump prices outside the authorized schedule.

Palace Press Officer Claire Castro confirmed the enforcement action on Sunday from New York, where President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. is attending the United Nations General Assembly. Castro said the stations must explain why their prices went up before the official pricing window, or face penalties including revocation of operating licenses.

This is the first mass enforcement action since the DOE ordered strict compliance with fuel pricing rules on March 7.

 WHAT THE GAS STATIONS DID

Under existing regulations, retail fuel stations are required to adjust prices only within scheduled pricing windows set by oil companies. Between those windows, pump prices are locked.

The 54 stations allegedly broke that rule. Multiple stations in various parts of the country raised diesel and gasoline prices before the next authorized adjustment, which is scheduled for Tuesday, March 10.

The show-cause orders require each station to submit a written explanation to the DOE. If they cannot justify the price increases, the department has authority under Presidential Decree 1865 and Republic Act 7581 (the Price Act) to suspend or revoke their operating permits, seize petroleum products involved in illegal stockpiling, and file criminal charges.

Energy Secretary Sharon Garin has maintained since last week that there is no fuel shortage in the country. The DOE, in coordination with the Department of the Interior and Local Government and the Philippine National Police, has deployed joint monitoring teams to gasoline stations nationwide.

 FROM NEW YORK, MARCOS FORMALIZES THE EMERGENCY POWERS REQUEST

On the same day the DOE moved against the 54 stations, Malacanan confirmed that President Marcos will formally submit a request to Congress for emergency powers to reduce the excise tax on petroleum products.

Marcos first announced the plan on March 4, when he said he was in discussions with Senate and House leaders about a mechanism to cut fuel excise taxes if Dubai crude oil prices exceeded $80 per barrel. That threshold was crossed within hours of the war's first day.

The formal request turns the March 4 announcement into a concrete legislative ask. Finance Secretary Frederick Go clarified that the emergency power would be a "precautionary measure" that the president may activate if necessary, not an automatic suspension.

Under Republic Act 10963, the Tax Reform for Acceleration and Inclusion (TRAIN) Law, excise taxes on fuel are supposed to be automatically suspended if the global average oil price hits $80 per barrel for three consecutive months. Senator Sherwin Gatchalian, chair of the Senate finance committee, has noted that this trigger provision expired in 2020 and was never renewed.

Several senators have already filed bills to grant the authority. Senator Joel Villanueva introduced Senate Bill No. 1922, which would authorize the president to suspend or reduce excise taxes on gasoline and diesel. Senator Bam Aquino filed Senate Bill No. 1923, which would grant emergency suspension powers during national and global crises.

 THE SUPPLY CRISIS JUST GOT WORSE

Across the Gulf, the situation that created the fuel crisis is deepening.

Kuwait Petroleum declared force majeure on Saturday — a legal clause releasing the company from contractual obligations — on all sales of crude oil and refinery products, according to a notice reported by Bloomberg and Reuters. Kuwait's production cut began at approximately 100,000 barrels per day and is projected to nearly triple by Sunday.

The United Arab Emirates has also begun reducing oil output. Both nations are responding to the near-total shutdown of shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow passage between Iran and Oman that carries approximately 20 percent of the world's crude oil and liquefied natural gas.

According to Reuters tracking data, tanker traffic through the strait dropped from 37 vessels on February 27 to zero by Wednesday, March 5. At least 200 vessels, including oil and LNG tankers, are now anchored in open waters outside the strait, unable to reach ports in Iraq, Saudi Arabia, and Qatar.

The Philippines imports approximately 90 percent of its oil from the Middle East. ING Bank economist Deepali Bhargava estimated that every 10 percent increase in oil prices could add up to 0.4 percentage points to Philippine inflation.

 WHAT COMES TUESDAY

The authorized fuel price adjustment scheduled for March 10 is expected to be severe.

DOE Oil Industry Management Bureau Director Rino Abad said Saturday that minimum projected increases are P19 per liter for diesel, P9 for gasoline, and P31 for kerosene. Those are floor estimates. They do not account for additional insurance premiums and risk surcharges driven by the Hormuz shipping freeze.

Brent crude crossed $100 per barrel last week.

The government has ordered oil companies to implement the increases in staggered phases rather than a single adjustment. Targeted fuel subsidies are being prepared for public utility vehicle drivers, farmers, and fishermen. The four-day compressed workweek adopted by all three branches of government is intended to reduce national fuel consumption.

Tuesday will be the first full pricing adjustment since the war began on February 28.

MARCOS IS IN NEW YORK TO ASK THE WORLD FOR PEACE WHILE 2 MILLION FILIPINOS WAIT IN A WAR ZONE

Where does a country with 2.4 million citizens scattered across the Middle East go when the region catches fire?

To the United Nations.

President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. departed Manila on Sunday, March 8, aboard a Philippine Air Force plane from Villamor Air Base at 10:22 a.m. He flew to New York for two days of engagements at the UN headquarters, March 9 and 10, with a delegation that includes First Lady Liza Araneta-Marcos, Foreign Affairs Secretary Maria Theresa Lazaro, Migrant Workers Secretary Hans Leo Cacdac, Presidential Adviser on Peace Carlito Galvez Jr., and Acting Presidential Communications Secretary Dave Gomez.

It is Marcos' 41st overseas trip since assuming office in 2022.

 WHAT HE WILL DO THERE

Two sessions. Two agendas.

Marcos will address a special session of the UN General Assembly where he is expected to call for restraint and adherence to international law, including the UN Charter and international humanitarian law. He will also urge all parties to the Iran conflict to return to negotiations for peace.

DFA spokesperson Angelica Escalona told reporters on Friday that the UN is "the most suitable platform" for Marcos to champion peace and the protection of all civilians, particularly Filipinos.

"The president aims to present a perspective from our region, representing developing nations, and will urge for restraint, adherence to international law, including the UN Charter and humanitarian principles, and for all parties to re-engage in negotiations for peace," she said.

Separately, Marcos will participate in the 70th session of the Commission on the Status of Women, where the Philippines will highlight its programs on the rights and welfare of women and girls.

He is also scheduled to meet with UN Secretary-General Antonio Guterres to discuss the latest developments in the Middle East and reaffirm Philippine commitment to multilateral cooperation.

 NO TRUMP MEETING, NO COMMUNITY EVENT

When asked whether Marcos would meet with US President Donald Trump, Escalona said no such meeting is on the agenda. No bilateral discussions with other world leaders are scheduled beyond the conversation with Guterres.

There will be no gathering with the Filipino community in New York.

The Philippines is in the middle of the most severe global oil disruption to affect its economy in years, driven in part by a war launched by the country Marcos is visiting. No bilateral with Trump is on the schedule despite the Philippines being one of the most directly affected non-combatant nations in the conflict.

 THE UNSC BID

Beyond the Middle East crisis, Marcos will use the General Assembly session to campaign for the Philippines' bid for a non-permanent seat on the UN Security Council for the 2027-2028 term. The election is scheduled for June 2026.

The Philippines has held a non-permanent seat four times since 1957, most recently from 2004 to 2005. DFA spokesperson Escalona said winning the seat would give the country a direct voice on global security decisions that affect national interests, maritime concerns, the economy, and the welfare of overseas Filipino workers.

Given the current crisis, a Security Council seat would give Manila a direct channel into the body that oversees international conflict and peacekeeping.

 THE BOARD OF PEACE QUESTION

There is one outstanding diplomatic item hanging over the trip.

Trump recently invited the Philippines to join his Board of Peace, a US-led initiative focused on the reconstruction of Gaza. Philippine Ambassador to the US Jose Manuel Romualdez confirmed that Trump sent a personal letter to Marcos, but Manila is still evaluating the nature of the commitment required.

Escalona declined to elaborate on the status of that proposal.

 WHAT IS AT STAKE BACK HOME

The Department of Foreign Affairs estimates 2.4 million Filipinos are living and working in the Middle East. As of last week, around 1,979 overseas Filipino workers across nine Gulf countries had requested repatriation. A first group of 299 Filipinos from Dubai arrived in the Philippines on the morning of March 6.

Airspace closures continue to delay evacuation flights. The Department of Migrant Workers on Saturday declared the Persian Gulf, the Strait of Hormuz, and the Gulf of Oman as "Warlike Operations Areas," allowing Filipino seafarers to legally refuse assignments in those waters.

One Filipino has been confirmed dead. Mary Ann Velasquez de Vera, a 32-year-old caregiver from Basista, Pangasinan, was killed by shrapnel from an Iranian missile in Tel Aviv on February 28, the first day of the war. She was protecting her elderly ward when the strike occurred.

Marcos will address the General Assembly on Monday. The Philippines has one confirmed civilian fatality, 2.4 million citizens in the conflict zone, and a fuel crisis already reshaping daily life back home. The UNSC election is in three months.

THE IRAN WAR JUST CROSSED INTO CIVILIAN INFRASTRUCTURE AND SAUDI ARABIA IS COUNTING ITS FIRST DEAD

Bahrain said an Iranian drone damaged a desalination plant on Sunday.

That is the first confirmed attack on a water treatment facility by any side in this war. Desalination plants supply drinking water to millions of people across the Gulf states, including hundreds of thousands of Filipino workers.

The strike on the Bahrain facility did not knock the water supply offline, according to the country's water authority. But the precedent it sets is what drew immediate alarm from regional governments and international observers.

Hours earlier, Iran accused the United States of striking a desalination plant on Qeshm Island in the Strait of Hormuz, cutting water to 30 villages. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said Washington "set this precedent, not Iran." US Central Command spokesperson Captain Tim Hawkins responded: "U.S. forces do not target civilians, period."

Both sides are now accusing each other of attacking civilian water infrastructure. The distinction between military and civilian targets has eroded.

 SAUDI ARABIA'S FIRST DEATHS

Saudi Arabia reported its first fatalities from the conflict on Sunday.

A military projectile struck a residential area, killing two people, one Indian national and one Bangladeshi national, according to the Saudi defense ministry. Twelve other Bangladeshi workers were wounded.

Foreign workers have made up the majority of casualties in Gulf states since the war began on February 28. In Israel, Filipino caregiver Mary Ann de Vera, 32, from Pangasinan, was killed by shrapnel from an Iranian missile in Tel Aviv on the first day of the war.

Bahrain, Kuwait, and the UAE reported additional Iranian missile and drone launches toward their territories on Sunday. The UAE said Iran had launched more than 100 missiles and drones in new barrages. Only four drones landed on Emirati soil, according to its defense ministry.

Kuwait intercepted multiple attacks. Qatar intercepted a ballistic missile on March 7. Bahrain, which hosts the US Navy's Fifth Fleet, has seen strikes on hotels, ports, and residential towers.

Since February 28, at least 18 people have been killed in Gulf states, 10 of them civilians, according to an AFP tally, in addition to at least 11 dead in Israel and more than 1,230 in Iran.

 IRAN REVERSES COURSE ON APOLOGY

On Saturday, Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian had offered an apology to Gulf neighbors, expressing regret for Iranian strikes on their soil. By Sunday, he had reversed himself.

"When we are attacked, we have no choice but to respond. The more pressure they impose on us, the stronger our response will naturally be," Pezeshkian said in a video statement. "Our Iran will not bow easily in the face of bullying, oppression, or aggression."

Judiciary chief Gholam Hossein Mohseni-Ejei had already contradicted the Saturday apology on X: "The geography of some countries in the region is in the hands of the enemy, and those points are used against our country. Intense attacks on these targets will continue."

Pezeshkian and Mohseni-Ejei sit on the three-member leadership council that has governed Iran since the death of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in a strike on February 28.

 WHAT BURNED IN TEHRAN

Israeli warplanes struck four oil storage tankers and a petroleum transfer terminal in Tehran overnight Saturday, killing four people, according to Iranian officials. Witnesses said the smoke was so dense it felt like the sun had not risen.

The Iranian Red Crescent Society said approximately 10,000 civilian structures across Iran have been damaged since the war began, including homes, schools, and nearly three dozen health facilities. It warned Tehran residents of toxic air pollution and the risk of acid rain.

Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Qalibaf warned Sunday that the war's impact on Iran's oil industry would continue to spiral, saying it could soon become harder to produce and sell oil.

 WHAT THIS MEANS FOR FILIPINOS IN THE GULF

There are approximately 2.4 million Filipinos living and working in the Middle East, according to the Department of Foreign Affairs. At least 1,979 OFWs in nine Gulf countries have requested repatriation as of last week.

The desalination plant attacks are a direct concern. Gulf states rely on these facilities for potable water. A sustained campaign against water infrastructure would threaten the basic survival of civilian populations, including migrant workers.

Airspace closures continue to delay repatriation flights. Dubai reopened briefly, allowing 299 Filipinos to return on March 6, but most Middle Eastern airspace remains restricted.

The Department of Migrant Workers on Saturday declared the Persian Gulf, the Strait of Hormuz, and the Gulf of Oman as "Warlike Operations Areas," allowing Filipino seafarers to legally refuse to sail those routes.

THE SUPREME COURT JUST JOINED THE 4-DAY WORKWEEK AND NOW ALL THREE BRANCHES OF GOVERNMENT ARE ON ENERGY CONSERVATION MODE

The judiciary did not wait long.

One day after President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. ordered a temporary four-day onsite work schedule for the executive branch, the Supreme Court issued its own memorandum circular. Starting Monday, March 9, all SC employees will follow a four-day onsite, one-day work-from-home arrangement.

First-level and second-level courts will do the same. Odd-numbered branches report onsite Monday through Thursday with Friday as a WFH day. Even-numbered branches report Tuesday through Friday with Monday off. The schedules alternate weekly, according to GMA News.

The legislature had already shifted to compressed schedules before the executive branch acted. Local government units across the country have also moved to abbreviated work weeks. With the judiciary now in, all three branches of the Philippine government are operating on reduced schedules for the first time in response to a single energy crisis.

 THE REASON IS FUEL

The trigger is the Iran war.

Marcos announced the executive branch shift on Friday, March 7, framing it as an energy conservation measure. Memorandum Circular No. 114 instructed all executive departments and agencies to cut electricity and fuel consumption by 10 to 20 percent. Non-essential government travel was banned. Meetings that could be held within offices were to replace external events.

The order exempted emergency and essential services, including police, firefighters, and frontline health workers.

Marcos was explicit about the cause. Global oil prices have surged because of the US-Israeli military campaign against Iran, which began on February 28 and has effectively shut down tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz. The Philippines imports nearly 90 percent of its oil from the Middle East.

The four-day arrangement remains in effect "until lifted or sooner revoked by the President," according to the circular.

 LGUS WERE ALREADY MOVING

Several local governments did not wait for Manila.

The Department of Trade and Industry shifted its Makati offices to a remote work setup starting March 13, citing the anticipated impact of Middle East fuel prices, according to the Inquirer.

Multiple LGUs across the country began implementing compressed work weeks in early March. The Inquirer reported on March 9 that the shift has now spread to regional and municipal government offices nationwide.

The Civil Service Commission had previously issued guidelines under Memorandum Circular No. 6 (s. 2022), modified by MC No. 1 (s. 2025), allowing flexible work arrangements. Marcos's new circular builds on that existing framework.

 LABOR GROUPS AND BUSINESS ARE PUSHING BACK

The four-day week is not without opposition.

The Philippine Chamber of Commerce and Industry warned on March 5 that a blanket implementation could "cripple output" in the country's industrial heartland, according to Manila Bulletin. PCCI president Perry Ferrer called for a "balanced and evidence-based" approach.

Ferrer argued that manufacturing, logistics, and export-dependent industries cannot simply compress their schedules without missing deadlines or breaching international delivery commitments. He pushed for a sector-sensitive framework with exemptions for industries that require 24/7 or six-day operations.

Labor groups also opposed the arrangement but for a different reason. Militant organizations described the scheme as "antiworker and antifamily," according to the Inquirer, arguing that compressed schedules increase daily work hours and commuting burdens without addressing the root cause of the crisis: fuel prices that workers cannot afford.

 WHAT IT MEANS FOR FILIPINOS GOING TO COURT

The judiciary's entry is the most practically significant shift.

Filipinos with pending cases, scheduled hearings, or legal deadlines must now navigate an alternating schedule. A case assigned to an odd-numbered branch will find that court closed on Fridays one week and Mondays the next.

The SC circular did not specify how this affects case filing deadlines, though the alternating system is designed to ensure that at least half of all courts remain open on any given weekday.

For the millions of Filipinos who interact with courts for everything from land titles to annulments, from labor disputes to criminal complaints, the compressed schedule adds a new layer of scheduling complexity to an already strained judicial system.

 THE SIGNAL

A four-day government work week is not a policy preference. When every branch of government simultaneously decides it can no longer afford to keep the lights on five days a week, that is a signal about the depth of the crisis.

Oil above $100 a barrel. A war with no end date. Fuel hikes projected to exceed P20 per liter on diesel by Tuesday. Emergency powers under negotiation. And now a government that has decided the best immediate response is to use less energy by working fewer days.

The arrangement is temporary. The question is how long temporary lasts.

CRUDE OIL JUST CROSSED $100 A BARREL AND THE BIGGEST FUEL PRICE HIKE IN PHILIPPINE HISTORY IS COMING ON TUESDAY

For nine days, the numbers climbed. Then they broke through.

Brent crude oil has surpassed $100 per barrel for the first time since the early days of the Russia-Ukraine war in 2022, driven by a military conflict that shows no signs of slowing.

On Monday, the Inquirer reported that global benchmark crude had crossed the triple-digit threshold as the US-Israeli offensive against Iran entered its tenth day. Tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow channel between Iran and Oman that carries roughly one-fifth of the world's oil supply, has collapsed to near zero, according to Reuters vessel tracking data from March 6. That chokepoint normally handles 15 million barrels of crude per day.

The Philippines imports nearly 90 percent of its oil from the Middle East. Every Filipino who drives to work, rides a jeepney, or buys food trucked in from the provinces will feel this at the pump on Tuesday.

 WHAT HITS ON TUESDAY

An industry source told the Inquirer on March 7 that diesel prices could surge by P20.10 to P20.30 per liter when the weekly fuel adjustment takes effect on March 10.

Gasoline is projected to spike by P10 to P10.20 per liter.

If these estimates hold, this would be the largest single-week increase in Philippine fuel prices in recent memory. Diesel, which was already climbing steeply, could approach P80 to P90 per liter, according to Philstar.

Energy Secretary Sharon Garin told reporters that some fuel companies had already begun rolling out preemptive hikes of P0.50 to P5 per liter to avoid one massive adjustment, according to ABS-CBN. She also warned the public against panic-buying or hoarding, saying it could "disrupt" the market. The government maintains the country has a two-month fuel supply.

 THE STRAIT THAT CONTROLS EVERYTHING

The Strait of Hormuz was not formally blockaded. Iran never declared it closed. But insurers withdrew coverage, ship owners pulled back, and the Revolutionary Guard Corps warned it would attack any vessel attempting to cross. By Wednesday, March 5, tanker crossings had dropped to zero, according to J.P. Morgan.

Wood Mackenzie called it a "dual supply shock." Exports were halted and OPEC's spare capacity was trapped behind the closed strait.

Saudi Arabia can redirect some volume via its East-West pipeline. The UAE has the Abu Dhabi pipeline. Together, roughly 6.5 million barrels per day, according to Rystad Energy. The strait normally carries more than double that.

Goldman Sachs estimated an $18-per-barrel risk premium already priced in. Barclays raised its Brent forecast to $100. Bloomberg reported traders warning that $100 was "imminent" if fighting continued.

 THE BSP PROBLEM

BSP Governor Eli Remolona warned last week that the bank may have to raise interest rates if oil prices stayed above $90, according to the Inquirer.

February inflation came in at 2.4 percent. ING Bank analyst Deepali Bhargava warned that a sustained 20 percent increase in oil could add 0.5 percentage points to headline inflation. Capital Economics projected that oil above $80 per barrel could add 50 basis points. With oil now above $100, the math becomes even more punishing.

The peso is under pressure too. MUFG projected the currency could slide to P59 against the dollar if crude holds at $90. At $100, the picture worsens. A weaker peso amplifies the inflation impact by raising the cost of imported crude in local currency terms.

The BSP had been on a rate-cutting path. That path is now in question. BPI senior economist Jun Neri cautioned that inflation could approach the upper limit of the BSP target range, and that the central bank may hold rates rather than cut.

 THE EMERGENCY POWERS PUSH

Congress is moving.

President Marcos told reporters on March 4 that he would seek emergency powers from Congress to reduce excise taxes on fuel products if Dubai crude stays above $80 per barrel. House Ways and Means Committee chairman Miro Quimbo filed House Bill 8257, which would give the President authority to suspend or lower petroleum excise during economic crises.

Separately, Navotas Representative Toby Tiangco proposed Joint House Resolution 4, calling for a halt in VAT collection on fuels during the crisis.

Senator Bam Aquino pointed out that the TRAIN law already has a provision for automatic suspension of fuel excise taxes when global oil prices exceed $80 per barrel for three consecutive months. But Palace Press Officer Claire Castro clarified that the TRAIN provision expired in 2020 and new legislation is needed.

Jetti Petroleum president Leo Bellas estimated that if the entire excise tax were suspended, diesel prices could drop by P6 per liter and gasoline by P10 per liter. The VAT component would further reduce the impact.

On Saturday, Senator Jan Almario Chan echoed the urgency, joining calls from both chambers for immediate action, according to Manila Bulletin.

 WHAT COMES NEXT

The Department of Energy has instructed fuel companies to implement gradual price adjustments rather than a single massive spike. But "gradual" has limits when the underlying commodity is rising this fast.

Filipino households that spend disproportionately on transportation and food, two categories deeply sensitive to fuel costs, will feel this immediately. Food prices track diesel costs because trucks and fishing boats run on diesel.

The Bangko Sentral is watching. Congress is scrambling. The Palace is negotiating emergency powers.

And the war is still in its second week.

THE CENTRAL BANK JUST WARNED IT MAY HAVE TO RAISE INTEREST RATES IF OIL HITS $100

Six months of monetary easing could be about to reverse.

In a CNBC interview over the weekend, Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas Governor Eli Remolona Jr. said the central bank might need to shift gears and raise interest rates if global crude oil prices climb to $100 per barrel and the US dollar keeps strengthening against the peso.

Neither scenario is hypothetical anymore. Oil prices surged 27 percent in a single week after US-Israel strikes on Iran paralyzed shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. The peso weakened to P58.44 against the dollar. February inflation jumped to a 13-month high of 2.4 percent.

The BSP has cut rates six times since August 2024, bringing its key policy rate down 225 basis points to a three-year low of 4.25 percent. That easing cycle was built to support an economy still dealing with the flood control corruption scandal and slow government spending.

Now the central bank faces a problem it did not expect this soon.

 NOMURA PULLS ITS RATE CUT FORECAST

Japanese investment bank Nomura has revised its Philippine inflation forecast for 2026 upward to 3.2 percent, from 2.5 percent.

More significantly, Nomura has retracted the final 25-basis-point rate cut it had predicted for April, now expecting the BSP to hold its policy rate steady for the foreseeable future.

"Although the BSP had previously indicated the possibility of further easing after implementing a 25-basis-point cut in February, it is evident that future decisions will be data-driven, particularly concerning inflation," Nomura stated.

Capital Economics, meanwhile, projects that crude oil above $80 per barrel could add 50 basis points to headline inflation. ING Bank analysts warned that a price shock "of this magnitude," combined with peso depreciation, could push inflation to the upper end of the BSP's 2 to 4 percent target range.

BPI lead economist Emilio Neri Jr. warned inflation could reach 4 percent in coming months, noting that higher oil prices would compound existing rice-driven pressures and produce second-round effects across transport, electricity, and logistics.

 THE REMITTANCE PROBLEM

Remolona also raised a concern that has received less attention than oil prices: the risk to overseas worker remittances.

Cash remittances from the Middle East totaled $6.5 billion in 2025, according to BSP data, roughly 18 percent of the Philippines' $35.6 billion total. With more than 2.4 million Filipinos working in the region, any sustained disruption to employment or income flows would be felt across the economy.

"There are some downside risks regarding the demand for our labor services. We are a significant exporter of labor services," Remolona told CNBC.

"While this is a concern, our top priority regarding Filipinos in the Middle East is to ensure their safety and potentially facilitate their return home."

Remolona called the Middle East remittance figure "very, very significant."

 A THREE-WAY COLLISION

The BSP now faces a convergence of pressures that work against each other.

Oil prices are rising, which pushes inflation up. The peso is weakening, which makes imported oil even more expensive. Remittance flows face disruption, which would further weaken the peso and reduce household income.

Raising rates would slow an already underperforming economy. GDP growth was 4.2 percent in 2025, well below the government's 5.5 to 6 percent target.

The import dependency makes it worse. The Philippines sources roughly 90 percent of its oil from the Middle East. Every 10 percent increase in oil prices adds an estimated 40 basis points to inflation and widens the current account deficit by about 60 basis points.

The Strait of Hormuz, where roughly 20 percent of global oil supply normally transits, has been effectively shut down since the conflict began on February 28. Iraq's oil production has collapsed by 70 percent. Kuwait declared force majeure. Qatar halted LNG production. Goldman Sachs raised its Brent crude forecast by $10 to $76 per barrel.

The Philippines does not produce enough of its own energy to cushion any of this.

 THE HOUSEHOLD SQUEEZE

If the BSP is forced to pause or reverse its easing cycle because of an external oil shock, the ripple effects are immediate. Loan rates stop falling. Business expansion slows. The peso stays under pressure.

For ordinary Filipino households, it would mean higher borrowing costs on top of already record fuel prices.

The fuel price adjustment expected on March 10 is projected to be the largest single-day increase in Philippine history. DOE projections put diesel at P22 to P24 per liter higher, gasoline at P11 to P13, kerosene at P33 to P36. Those are increases, not new prices.

The BSP cut rates specifically to support an economy weakened by the flood control corruption scandal and sluggish government spending. GDP growth came in at 4.2 percent in 2025 — well below target.

Whether the central bank still has room to keep easing, or whether the war in the Middle East has closed that door, depends on what oil does next.

IRAN'S ASSEMBLY OF EXPERTS SAYS IT HAS CHOSEN A NEW SUPREME LEADER AS THE WAR ENTERS DAY 10

The vote has been cast.

Iran's Assembly of Experts, the 88-member clerical body constitutionally tasked with selecting the country's supreme leader, announced on Sunday that it has reached a decision on who will succeed the late Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.

The name has not been formally announced. But the clues are not subtle.

"The name of Khamenei will continue," Assembly member Hosseinali Eshkevari said in a video carried by Iranian media. "With the majority of votes, the person who will continue the path of Imam Khomeini and the path of the martyr Imam Khamenei has been chosen. The vote has been cast and will be announced soon."

Another member, Mohsen Heydari, told Iran's ISNA news agency that "the most suitable candidate, approved by the majority of the Assembly of Experts, has been determined."

Ahmad Alamolhoda, a senior cleric, went further. He said all reports suggesting the assembly had not yet decided were "completely false," adding that the head of the assembly's secretariat, Ayatollah Hashem Hosseini Bushehri, is now responsible for making the announcement public, according to Iran's Mehr news agency.

 THE STRONGEST SIGNAL YET

A separate Assembly member, Ayatollah Mohsen Heidari Alekasir, offered what may be the most telling statement. He said the chosen successor was picked based on the late supreme leader's own criteria: that Iran's top leader should "be hated by the enemy" rather than admired by it.

"Even the Great Satan has mentioned his name," Heidari Alekasir said, according to Al Jazeera.

That remark came days after US President Donald Trump told reporters that Mojtaba Khamenei, the second son of the assassinated leader, was the most likely successor. Trump called it an "unacceptable" outcome and said he wanted to be personally involved in choosing Iran's next leader, according to a report by Axios.

Mojtaba Khamenei, a mid-ranking hardline cleric who has never held elected office, has long been considered the frontrunner. Iran International, citing informed sources, reported that the Revolutionary Guards had pushed for his appointment to be finalized quickly, even outside the legally prescribed procedures, given the ongoing airstrikes that have made a physical meeting of the assembly impossible.

 THE DELAY

The formal announcement has been held up by a procedural dispute and a security calculation.

Some Assembly members believe the decision requires an in-person gathering. Others argue that meeting physically is too dangerous while American and Israeli warplanes continue to operate freely over Iranian airspace. Israel struck the building where the Assembly of Experts was meeting on Tuesday, according to Israeli sources who spoke to the Jerusalem Post.

The security fear runs deeper than procedure. The New York Times reported, citing unnamed officials, that the announcement has been delayed because Iranian authorities believe the new leader will be immediately targeted by Israel once his identity is confirmed.

Israel has given those fears weight. A spokesperson for the Israeli military said on Sunday that Israel would "pursue every successor" to Khamenei, according to multiple international outlets.

 THE WAR ON DAY 10

The leadership announcement comes as the US-Israeli air campaign enters its tenth day with no sign of slowing.

According to Al Jazeera, citing Iran's health ministry, more than 1,300 people have been killed in Iran since February 28. Israel said over the weekend that it had struck 300 targets in Iran. CBS News reported that since the war began, Israel has conducted approximately 3,400 strikes and dropped roughly 7,500 munitions.

On Saturday, the air campaign escalated to oil infrastructure for the first time, according to Iran's Fars News Agency, with strikes on four oil storage facilities and one oil production transfer center in and around Tehran. On Sunday, Israeli strikes reportedly targeted an oil depot near Qeshm Airport in southern Iran, close to the Strait of Hormuz itself, according to social media reports that were corroborated by satellite imagery circulated by open-source intelligence analysts.

The Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly one-fifth of the world's oil passes, remains largely blocked. Reuters reported on Friday that tanker crossings had dropped to zero, down from 37 vessels on February 27. Some limited cargo movement resumed on Day 8 after Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian apologized to neighboring Gulf states for Iranian retaliatory strikes that damaged civilian infrastructure in Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Bahrain, and Qatar, according to Al Jazeera.

Goldman Sachs warned that global oil prices could breach the $100 per barrel mark within days and reach $150 per barrel by the end of the month without a resolution to the shipping disruption, according to a report cited by the financial news aggregator ConflictLive.

 WHAT THIS MEANS FOR THE PHILIPPINES

The connection between a clerical succession vote in Tehran and the price of diesel in Quezon City is direct.

Oil product prices in the Philippines are set to spike by P18 to P20 per liter on Tuesday, March 10, according to the Department of Energy as reported by the Inquirer and Philstar. If the full projected hike takes effect, diesel could reach P80 to P90 per liter.

The naming of a new supreme leader could prolong the conflict. If the Assembly confirms Mojtaba Khamenei and Israel follows through on its stated intention to target him, the war enters a phase where neither side has an obvious exit. That means the Strait of Hormuz stays disrupted. That means oil keeps climbing. That means every Filipino commuter, farmer, and fisherfolk pays the price.

Approximately 1.5 million land-based OFWs remain in the Middle East, according to DMW data. As of March 4, more than 1,400 have requested repatriation. A total of 2,012 have been served by the DMW, according to the Philippine News Agency. The House of Representatives has ordered a joint congressional briefing on OFW safety for March 11.

President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. departed Sunday for a three-day work visit to the United States, where he will address the UN General Assembly and call for peace in the Middle East, according to Philstar.
